title: Spatially resolved profiling of extracellular vesicles in tissues with Spatial-EV-seq - Nature Biotechnology
text:
AbstractExtracellular vesicles (EVs) have emerged as promising biomarkers for monitoring physiological homeostasis and pathological progression. However, current analytic methods face limitations in preserving spatial information about EVs and their intricate connections to parental and recipient cells. Here, we present Spatial-EV-seq, a method for in situ spatial profiling of EVs within their native microenvironmental context. Spatial-EV-seq uses an antibody-engineered capture interface to preserve EVs’ spatial distribution, followed by rolling circle amplification with EV surface-binding aptamers, enabling fluorescence imaging and molecular profiling of individual EVs. The method integrates ultrasensitive EV profiling, molecular subtyping and high-resolution spatial mapping with transcriptomics to resolve location-specific EV–cell communication networks. In an anti-PD1-treated breast cancer mouse model, we uncover a spatially orchestrated immunosuppressive axis: PDL1+ EV-enriched zones drive CD8+ T cell dysfunction, establishing immune-privileged niches, whereas PDL1+ EV-depleted regions preserve immunocompetence and therapeutic sensitivity. Spatial-EV-seq offers insights into EV-mediated mechanisms and unlocks avenues for precision diagnostics and therapeutics.

Data availability

Sequence data supporting the findings of this study were deposited to the Genome Sequence Archive under accession number CRA039683. Source data are provided with this paper.
Code availability

Source code related to this manuscript is available from GitHub (https://github.com/BuckyEv/Spatial-EV-seq).

The samples without EVs and without RCA reaction were used as control groups. b, The co-localization study of the CD63 EV@RCA and pre-membrane-stained exogenous EVs. c, The co-localization study of the PDL1 EV@RCA and pre-membrane-stained exogenous EVs. d, The statistical analysis of co-localization rates between CD63 EV@RCA or PDL1 EV@RCA signals and pre-membrane-stained exogenous EVs. Co-localization rate was calculated as the proportion of RCA-derived signal spots that overlapped with membrane-stained EV signals. n = 3 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d. Created in BioRender. Wen, Q. (2026) https://BioRender.com/yb06vje.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 2 The quantification ability of Spatial-EV-seq.a, Fluorescence images generated by Spatial-EV-seq with different EV inputs. Scale bar: 10 μm. n = 5 biological replicates. Additional figures are presented in Fig. 2f. b, Line-intensity profiles illustrating three spatial separation scenarios used to evaluate the resolving power of EV puncta segmentation. Three representative configurations were selected: well-resolved, overlapped but resolvable, and unresolved. For each configuration, a representative fluorescence image is shown (pixel size = 0.324 μm), and a manually drawn analysis line is indicated by a yellow arrow. The intensity profile along the line was then extracted from the corresponding fluorescence RGB channel (s). Intensities were calculated as the mean pixel value within a 5-pixel-wide band centered on the analysis line and sampled at 500 evenly spaced positions (x-axis, distance along the line in pixels; y-axis, grayscale intensity, 0-255). The well-resolved case shows two distinct peaks separated by a valley approaching baseline. The overlapped-but-resolvable case exhibits partial overlap in the image but still yields two discernible peaks and a detectable local minimum, enabling separation by the segmentation strategy. The unresolved case presents a single broadened peak or lacks a reliable local minimum, resulting in a merged signal cluster. c, Percentage of well-resolved as well as overlapped but resolvable signal spots across EV inputs within the linear detection range. d, Percentage of unresolved signal spots across EV inputs within the linear detection range. c and d, n = 5 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d. Each point represents one image; centre lines indicate medians, box bounds denote the 25th and 75th percentiles, and whiskers extend to values within 1.5× the interquartile range. Numbers above groups indicate mean values.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 3 Characterization of kidney tissue-derived EVs capture.a, The kidney-derived EVs isolated from the medium of 24-hour kidney-embedded cultures. b, SEM images of kidney tissue-derived EVs on the Capture Surface. c, Wide-field TEM image of particles released from the Capture Surface. d, EV@RCA on the Capture Surface. e, Flow cytometry analysis of the binding ability of CD63 aptamer-L, EpCAM aptamer-L and PDL1 aptamer-L to kidney tissue-derived EVs. f, Fluorescence images of CD63, EpCAM, and PDL1 protein in the mouse kidney tissue-derived EVs detected by Spatial-EV-seq. Scale bar: 10 µm. g, Fluorescence images of EpCAM protein of tissue EVs from 4T1-bearing tumors. RS-apt refers to the use of random sequence instead of aptamers. Scale bar: 10 µm. Created in BioRender. Wen, Q. (2026) https://BioRender.com/2w25ixh.Extended Data Fig. 4 Schematic of the blotting procedure.80 µm frozen tissue sections were prepared by cryostat and mounted on slides for handling. And 80 µm double-sided adhesive spacer matching the tissue section thickness was attached to the tissue slide around the tissue section. a, The slide with tissue section was brought into direct, uniform, gap-free contact with the Capture Surface slide under controlled temperature to ensure consistent EV transfer. All pre-blotting steps were performed at low temperature to maintain the frozen state and minimize EV diffusion. During blotting, the temperature was elevated to 37 °C to promote EV release and capture. b, Blotting by using a custom-built blotting instrument. The Capture Surface was mounted on the inner side of the instrument lid, while slide with tissue sections was positioned in the sample chamber. Closing the lid brought the two surfaces into precise and uniform contact, respectively. Then the instrument lid was lowered to bring the Capture Surface into tight contact for consistent EV transfer and capturing. Created in BioRender. Wen, Q. (2026) https://BioRender.com/wy840ae.Extended Data Fig. 5 Characterization of in situ capture and profiling performance of Capture Surface.a-d, Assessment of EV displacement during transfer for additional two experiments. a and c, Spatial distributions of exogenous fluorescent EVs in tissue sections pre-blotting, on the Capture Surface post-blotting and the merge image. b and d, Diffusion distance quantification. Lateral displacement was calculated based on the positional differences of individual EVs between pre- and post-blotting images. Each pixel corresponds to 0.324 μm. e, Staining with antibodies against organelle-specific and extracellular membrane markers. After capturing EVs from the tissue on the Capture Surface, the surface was stained with antibodies against organelle-specific proteins, including Calnexin (endoplasmic reticulum), GM130 (Golgi apparatus), Lamin A/C (nuclear envelope), and LAMP1 (lysosome), as well as the transmembrane protein desmoglein 1 (non-EV-derived membrane protein). The corresponding tissue sections were also stained with the same antibodies. f, The RCA-mediated detection signal on anti-CD81, anti-CD63, anti-CD9 and anti-IgG functionalized Capture Surface. g, Immobilization and detection of exogenous fluorescent EVs on the Capture Surface. Left: direct immobilization of exogenous fluorescent EVs; middle: detection of CD63+ EVs via RCA amplification; right: merged image. h, Quantification of detection efficiency across tissue samples was performed by calculating the ratio of CD63 RCA signal count to the number of PKH26-labeled EVs within the same imaging field. n = 3 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 6 Location heterogeneity of EVs in cancer model.a, H&E staining results of tumor tissue, peritumoral tissue, metastatic tumor of lung from tumor-bearing mice, and lung tissue from healthy mouse. b, Multiplexed detection of EVs in tumor tissue, peritumoral tissue, metastatic tumor of lung and healthy lung. c, Representative images of CD63, PDL1, EpCAM, CSV, CD62L, Siglec-15-expressed EV distribution on the CD63 antibody Capture Surface in tumor tissues from 4T1-bearing mice with/without PD1 antibody treatment. Scale bar: 10 μm. d, Statistical analysis of PDL1, EpCAM, CSV, CD62L, Siglec-15-expressed EV numbers on the CD63 antibody Capture Surface in 4T1-bearing mice with/without PD1 antibody treatment. Data were normalized by the CD63 protein expression within the same field of view. n = 10 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d. e, Cluster analysis of 4T1-bearing mice with/without PD1 antibody treatment on the CD63 antibody Capture Surface. f, As in (c), but with CD9 antibody-functionalized Capture Surface. Scale bar: 10 μm. g, As in (d), with CD9 antibody-functionalized Capture surface. Data were normalized to CD63 protein expression within the same field of view. n = 10 biological replicates. Bars: mean ± s.d. h, As in (e), with CD9 antibody-functionalized Capture Surface.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 7 Joint analysis of Spatial EV group and spatial transcriptome in tumor tissues before immunotherapy.a, The Gene Ontology biological process analysis on the CD8+ T cells in high-density PDL1+ EV regions (Cluster 1) and low-density PDL1+ EV regions (Cluster 2). b, The spatial distribution of the typical ligand-receptor interactions in Cluster 1 before treatment. c, The spatial distribution of the typical ligand-receptor interactions in Cluster 2 before treatment. d, Heatmap visualising sender-receiver of cell type interactions in Cluster 1. e, Heatmap visualising sender-receiver of cell type interactions in Cluster 2.Source dataExtended Data Fig. 8 Clinical hepatocellular carcinoma biopsy specimens detected by Spatial-EV-seq.The square profiling area is 0.75 cm × 0.75 cm. n = 1 sample. a, The H&E images. b, The cell annotation. c, Distribution of PDL1+ EV. d, Distribution of ICAM1+ EV. e, Distribution of CD8+ T cell. f, Distribution of NK cell. g, Ligand-receptor-mediated interactions between spatially neighboring cell types in high-PDL1+ EV (Cluster 1). h, Ligand-receptor-mediated interactions between spatially neighboring cell types in low-PDL1 + EV (Cluster 2). i, Cell-type communication networks (sender-receiver) in high-PDL1 + EV
